reasoning: The nonce action reuse is confirmed in code: both class.admin.php:195 and class.contact-data.php:121 call wp_verify_nonce(..., MWF_Config::NAME) with the identical action string 'mw-wp-form', and both templates (admin/url.php:9 and contact-data/detail.php:9) call wp_create_nonce(MWF_Config::NAME) identically. Cross-handler replay of the nonce token is therefore technically possible for the same authenticated user. However, the finding's reachability claim fails adversarial scrutiny: (1) Both handlers require current_user_can('edit_pages') — any user who can reach one can already reach the other with legitimate intent; there is no privilege escalation. (2) WordPress nonces are user-session-bound — an attacker on a different origin cannot read the victim's nonce value, so this does not enable CSRF for unauthenticated or lower-privileged users. (3) The only 'attack' enabled is that a user with edit_pages can replay their own nonce from one save handler to another — but they already have full authorization for both operations. No post they could not otherwise edit becomes accessible. The pattern is a code-quality defect (violates WordPress nonce scoping best practice) but not an exploitable vulnerability under any realistic attacker model. Severity is at most informational/low, not medium. Verdict: not a real security finding by the default-false standard.
